frameworks.name: public goods game
frameworks.one_line: virtual-world economic game embedding a collective intelligence task

frameworks.name: collective intelligence
frameworks.one_line: information aggregation across many contributors for group decisions

frameworks.name: collective action / free-rider problem
frameworks.one_line: costly contribution dilemma where non-contributors benefit from others' input

core_question: Does incentivizing free riders to participate improve collective intelligence relative to relying only on intrinsically motivated contributors?
key_assumptions: individual contributions to collective intelligence tasks are costly
intrinsic motivation alone produces a selectively biased contributor pool
information aggregation accuracy depends on contributor diversity and pool size
manipulations: incentive scheme: incentivize all participants vs. rely only on intrinsically motivated contributors
embedding of a collective intelligence task within a large-scale virtual-world public goods game
key_findings: incentivizing all participants (including free riders) increased information aggregation accuracy
selective participation by intrinsically motivated agents alone yielded lower collective accuracy
interpretations: selective participation reduces collective intelligence by shrinking the contributor pool and its information diversity
intrinsically motivated agents are not necessarily the best participants in collective decisions
